title: Sarcoidosis
oneLiner: Multisystem granulomatous disease of unknown cause, classically in young Black women and Scandinavians, often with lung/hilar involvement but can affect any organ.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 32-year-old Black female presents with a 2-week history of dry cough, breathlessness on climbing stairs, and vague chest discomfort. She also complains of red, painful eyes and tender red bumps on her shins. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) and clear lung fields. She is otherwise well and not febrile. On examination, you note erythema nodosum, bilateral anterior uveitis, and no crackles on auscultation. ACE level is raised. She mentions she recently stopped taking her contraceptive pill, which is irrelevant.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**
- **Demographics (young Black female)** → Sarcoidosis has highest incidence in Black Americans and people of Scandinavian descent; female predominance; peak age 20-40.
- **Dry cough + breathlessness** → Interstitial lung involvement or airway irritation from granulomatous inflammation; early stage often no crackles.
- **Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) on CXR** → Classic and near-pathognomonic; symmetrical "potato nodes" on CXR.
- **Erythema nodosum (EN)** → Tender red nodules on shins – a common acute presentation (Löfgren syndrome when combined with BHL and arthralgia/uveitis).
- **Red painful eyes (anterior uveitis)** → Granulomatous inflammation of uveal tract; can be bilateral.
- **Raised ACE** → Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme elevated in ~60% of active sarcoidosis; not specific but supportive.
- **No fever, well appearance** → Unlike infection; sarcoidosis often presents in a relatively well patient despite significant radiological findings.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Cardiac sarcoidosis** → Young adult with syncope, heart block, or ventricular arrhythmias; often misdiagnosed as myocarditis or inherited cardiomyopathy. Look for late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI.
- **Neurosarcoidosis** → Cranial nerve palsy (especially facial nerve), meningeal enhancement, or hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction. Can mimic multiple sclerosis or stroke.
- **Isolated skin sarcoidosis** → Chronic plaques, lupus pernio (red/purple nodules on nose/ears), or scar sarcoid (infiltration of old scars). Patient may have no other symptoms – easy to miss.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory disorder characterized by non-caseating epithelioid granulomas in affected organs. The lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes are involved in >90% of cases, but virtually any organ can be affected (eyes, skin, heart, nervous system, liver, spleen, bones). The cause remains unknown, though it's thought to be an exaggerated immune response to an environmental antigen in genetically susceptible individuals.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Annual incidence ~10-40/100,000 in the US; higher in Black populations (3x-4x). It's a common cause of interstitial lung disease.
- **Stakes:** Most patients (~60%) have self-limiting disease, but up to 30% develop chronic progressive sarcoidosis leading to pulmonary fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension, or end-organ damage (e.g., cardiac sarcoidosis causing sudden death).
- **Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosis as tuberculosis or lymphoma is common due to similar presentation with BHL and granulomas. Conversely, missing cardiac sarcoidosis can be fatal. Also, corticosteroids are mainstay but overtreatment can cause harm; many cases resolve spontaneously.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** Sarcoidosis begins when an unknown antigen (possible triggers: mycobacteria, propionibacteria, inorganic dusts) is presented to CD4+ T-cells in genetically predisposed individuals. The CD4+ T-cells (primarily Th1/Th17) release interferon-gamma and IL-2, which recruit macrophages. Macrophages accumulate and differentiate into epithelioid cells, fusing into multinucleated giant cells, forming **non-caseating granulomas** — the histological hallmark. Unlike tuberculosis, there is no central necrosis (caseation) in sarcoid granulomas.

**Mechanism -> Clinical signs:**
- **Granuloma formation in lungs** → Compromises alveolar architecture, causing restrictive lung disease, dry cough, dyspnea. Early: no crackles because granulomas are interstitial, not alveolar filling.
- **Hilar and mediastinal lymph node involvement** → BHL on CXR. Granulomas obstruct lymphatic drainage, causing node enlargement. Symmetrical because both lungs are exposed to same antigens.
- **Erythema nodosum** → Immune complex deposition or direct granulomatous inflammation in subcutaneous fat; mediated by TNF-α and other cytokines. Typically acute onset in Löfgren syndrome.
- **Uveitis** → Direct granulomatous infiltration of uveal tract; anterior uveitis is most common, causing photophobia, redness, pain. Granulomas disrupt the blood-ocular barrier.
- **Elevated ACE** → Granulomatous epithelioid cells produce ACE; serum levels correlate with total body granuloma burden but not specific (also ↑ in TB, Gaucher disease).
- **Anergy (negative tuberculin skin test)** → Paradoxical; sarcoidosis patients often have diminished delayed-type hypersensitivity (due to Th1 compartment quiescence?), while simultaneously having hyperactive granulomatous inflammation.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The well-appearing patient with BHL, EN, and uveitis reflects a robust but organized granulomatous reaction – hence "Löfgren syndrome" often has excellent prognosis. Conversely, chronic progressive sarcoidosis (fibrotic lung, lupus pernio) suggests dysregulated inflammation with fibrosis.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**Most common cause: Idiopathic** – by definition, no definite trigger identified. But associations exist.

| Category | Specific trigger | Stem clue / presentation |
|----------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | No known cause (majority) | Classic BHL + EN in young woman |
| **Genetic / Familial** | HLA-DRB1*0301, BTNL2 variants | Scandinavian ancestry, family history of sarcoidosis |
| **Infectious (proposed)** | Mycobacteria (M. tuberculosis, M. avium) | Patient from high TB area – but sarcoid granulomas lack caseation and stain negative for acid-fast bacilli. "Fungal" triggers also suggested (P. acnes) |
| **Environmental / Occupational** | Exposures: pine pollen, beryllium, aluminum, zirconium | Occupational history – be aware of berylliosis (disease mimics sarcoidosis but specifically from beryllium; lymphocytes show proliferation to beryllium on blood test) |
| **Drug-induced** | TNF-α inhibitors (paradoxical sarcoidosis), immune checkpoint inhibitors | New-onset sarcoid-like reactions in patients on anti-TNF or PD-1 inhibitors |

**Key point:** In clinical practice, you rarely identify a specific trigger – hence "idiopathic" remains the default. However, if the patient has occupational exposure to beryllium, you must differentiate chronic beryllium disease (CBD) from sarcoidosis via a beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT) – CBD is indistinguishable histologically.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** No single gold standard. Diagnosis requires: (1) compatible clinical presentation, (2) histologic evidence of non-caseating granulomas (from biopsy of most accessible affected site), (3) exclusion of other causes of granulomas (TB, fungal, berylliosis, etc.). The **WASOG criteria** (World Association of Sarcoidosis and Other Granulomatous Diseases) classify as "definite" if histology + typical imaging.

**Chest X-ray (first line):** Shows Scadding stages:
- **Stage 0:** Normal
- **Stage I:** BHL only
- **Stage II:** BHL + interstitial lung disease (reticulonodular opacities)
- **Stage III:** Interstitial lung disease without BHL (woody fibrosis)
- **Stage IV:** Pulmonary fibrosis (honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis)

**Pattern recognition:** Stage I is classic in acute Löfgren – good prognosis (60-80% spontaneous resolution). Stage IV indicates irreversible fibrosis, poor prognosis.

**High-resolution CT chest:** Shows perilymphatic nodules (along fissures and bronchovascular bundles) – "cluster of grapes" sign. Also ground-glass opacities, hilar lymphadenopathy. Helps guide biopsy.

**Biopsy (essential for definitive diagnosis):** Options:
- Bronchoscopy with endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS)-guided TBNA of hilar nodes (first choice, high yield)
- Transbronchial lung biopsy (yield ~70%)
- Endobronchial biopsy (if visible mucosal lesions)
- Skin biopsy of EN or lupus pernio (easy if skin involved)
- Conjunctival biopsy (if ocular symptoms)

**Histology:** Non-caseating epithelioid granulomas with Schaumann bodies (laminated concretions) and asteroid bodies (stellate inclusions) – classic but not specific.

**Serum ACE:** Elevated in ~60% of active sarcoidosis. Not diagnostic alone (↑ in TB, lymphoma, Gaucher, hyperthyroidism). Useful for monitoring disease activity – falling ACE may indicate response to therapy.

**Calcium metabolism:** 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D is produced by granulomas (macrophages have 1α-hydroxylase). This leads to hypercalciuria in up to 40% and hypercalcemia in ~10%. **Check serum calcium and 24-hour urine calcium.** High calcium can cause nephrocalcinosis.

**Pulmonary function tests (PFTs):** Restrictive pattern (↓ FVC, ↓ TLC, normal or ↑ FEV1/FVC). DLCO may be reduced early due to parenchymal involvement.

**Other labs:** 
- Elevated ESR/CRP (non-specific)
- Lymphocytopenia (common)
- Hypergammaglobulinemia (polyclonal)
- Negative ANCA, negative ANA (unless co-existing autoimmune disease)

**Single finding that confirms:** Non-caseating granuloma on biopsy + negative cultures and stains for AFB/fungi **in the right clinical context**.

**Single finding that rules out:** Positive AFB smear/culture from same sample (TB) or identification of beryllium-specific hypersensitivity.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – if severe/exacerbation:**
- Usually not an emergency unless:
  - **Hypercalcemic crisis** (Ca >3.5 mmol/L, altered mental state, ECG short QT): IV normal saline 1-2L, bisphosphonates (pamidronate 60-90mg IV over 2-4h) consultation with endocrinology.
  - **Cardiac sarcoidosis with ventricular arrhythmia or heart block:** Immediate cardiology consult, monitor in CCU, consider temporary pacing if high-grade AV block. No specific anti-inflammatory within first hour unless electrophysiology guided.
- **Acute uveitis:** Urgent ophthalmology referral – topical steroids (prednisolone acetate 1% drops q1-2h) + cycloplegics.

**Ward (24-72h) – for newly diagnosed or exacerbation:**
- **Indications for systemic therapy:** Symptomatic disease (cough, dyspnea, fatigue, significant eye/skin/heart involvement) or progressive pulmonary function decline.
- **First line:** Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 40-80mg) oral for 4-6 weeks, then taper gradually over 6-12 months. Steroids reduce granuloma burden and suppress cytokine release.
- **Why not start immediate steroids in all?** Many patients remit spontaneously (especially Stage I). Unnecessary steroids cause weight gain, osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts. Use shared decision-making.
- **Other acute measures:** Calcium restricted diet if hypercalcemia; IVF if high calcium. Monitor renal function.
- **When to add second-line:**
  - Refractory disease / steroid side effects: **Methotrexate** 7.5-25mg weekly oral/subcut (with folic acid 5mg weekly). Check LFTs, FBC, creatinine.
  - **Hydroxychloroquine** 200-400mg/day for skin/calcium issues.
  - **Infliximab** 5mg/kg IV at weeks 0,2,6 then q8w for severe refractory.

**Long-term (months to years):**
- Taper steroids to lowest effective dose (often ≤10mg prednisolone/day). Continue methotrexate or other steroid-sparing agent.
- **Pulmonary fibrosis:** Pulmonary rehabilitation, long-term oxygen if PaO₂ <55mmHg, consider lung transplant if end-stage.
- **Cardiac sarcoidosis:** Long-term immunosuppression (steroids ± mycophenolate) plus implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) if significant arrhythmias or LVEF <35%.
- **Ocular sarcoidosis:** Long-term topical steroids, sometimes systemic. Biannual slit-lamp exams.
- **Hypercalcemia:** Avoid vitamin D supplements, limit sun exposure, consider hydroxychloroquine (reduces 1,25-(OH)2D production).
- **Monitoring:** Every 6-12 months: CXR, PFTs, ACE, calcium, ophthalmology review. Image for cardiac/neuro symptoms.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- Do NOT start steroids for asymptomatic Stage I BHL alone – 50-70% resolve spontaneously.
- Do NOT use TNF-α inhibitors (especially etanercept) – they can actually cause sarcoidosis flares! Infliximab is preferred for refractory cases.
- Do NOT stop steroids abruptly – leads to rebound granulomatous inflammation.
- Do NOT assume a raised ACE confirms sarcoidosis in a patient with chest symptoms from lymphoma or TB – always get tissue.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Sarcoidosis | Tuberculosis | Lymphoma | Berylliosis |
|---------|-------------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Usually no chest pain (if pulmonary) | Pleuritic chest pain possible | Chest pain rare unless large nodes | Similar to sarcoidosis; no pain |
| **Exam** | Crackles late; erythema nodosum; uveitis; clubbing uncommon | Night sweats, fever, weight loss (classic TB symptoms); crackles | Supraclavicular nodes (Rubber, fixed); hepatosplenomegaly; fever | Crackles with interstitial disease; no EN |
| **CXR** | BHL symmetrical "potato nodes"; interstitial nodules | Usually unilateral hilar or paratracheal node; apical cavities | Bilateral but often asymmetrical hilar/mediastinal nodes; ± pulmonary masses | BHL and reticulonodular opacities identical to sarcoidosis |
| **Labs** | ACE ↑; Ca ↑; negative TST/IGRA | Positive TST/IGRA (if not anergic); sputum AFB positive | Elevated LDH; flow cytometry shows clonal lymphocytes | BeLPT positive; ACE may be ↑ |
| **Key diff** | Non-caseating granuloma + negative cultures | Caseating granuloma + AFB+ | Lymphoma cells (Reed-Sternberg in Hodgkin) | Positive beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test; occupational history |

**Additional mimics:** 
- **Hypersensitivity pneumonitis:** No EN, no uveitis, no BHL; history of bird/occupational dust exposure; precipitating antibodies.
- **Churg-Strauss (EGPA):** Eosinophilia, asthma, vasculitic rash, p-ANCA positive.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Pulmonary fibrosis (Stage IV):**
- **Presentation:** Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, digital clubbing (late). CXR shows honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis.
- **Key signs:** Bilateral basal crackles, reduced FVC and DLCO.
- **What to do:** High-resolution CT to assess fibrosis extent. Start long-term oxygen if hypoxic. Lung transplant evaluation. Stop smoking.
- **If untreated:** Irreversible respiratory failure, pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale, death.

**2. Cardiac sarcoidosis:**
- **Presentation:** Palpitations, syncope, heart failure, sudden cardiac death (may be first presentation). ECG: AV block (any degree), bundle branch block, ventricular ectopy, Q waves simulating MI.
- **Key signs:** Abnormal cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement (mid-myocardial, not subendocardial). PET shows focal FDG uptake.
- **What to do:** Start high-dose steroids (prednisolone 1mg/kg) + methotrexate. Insert ICD if LVEF ≤35% or significant arrhythmias.
- **If missed:** Sudden death is common – cardiac sarcoidosis is a leading cause of death in sarcoidosis patients.

**3. Neurosarcoidosis:**
- **Presentation:** Cranial nerve palsy (CN VII most common), headache, seizures, spinal cord symptoms. MRI: leptomeningeal enhancement, periventricular white matter lesions.
- **Key signs:** CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, ACE may be ↑. Rule out infectious meningitis.
- **What to do:** High-dose steroids (IV methylprednisolone 1g/day for 5 days) then oral taper. Add immunosuppressant (methotrexate, mycophenolate).
- **If missed:** Permanent cranial nerve deficits, hydrocephalus, myelopathy.

**4. Hypercalcemia / Nephrolithiasis:**
- **Presentation:** Polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, confusion. Stones may present as colicky flank pain.
- **Key signs:** Serum Ca >2.6 mmol/L; urine Ca high; nephrocalcinosis on ultrasound.
- **What to do:** Hydration, bisphosphonates if severe, stop vitamin D, consider hydroxychloroquine.
- **If missed:** Renal failure, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

**5. Ocular sarcoidosis (chronic):**
- **Presentation:** Gradual vision loss, glaucoma, cataracts. Posterior uveitis can cause macular edema.
- **What to do:** Regular ophthalmology follow-up. Topical/systemic steroids. Consider anti-TNF if refractory.
- **If missed:** Blindness.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **LÖFGREN syndrome** (good prognosis):  
  L – Lymphadenopathy (bilateral hilar)  
  Ö – Ögon (eye = uveitis)  
  F – Fever (low-grade)  
  G – Granulomatous arthritis (ankle pain/swelling)  
  R – Rash (erythema nodosum)  
  EN – Erythema Nodosum (already covered)

- **CARDIAC sarcoidosis clues:**  
  C – Conduction defects (AV block)  
  A – Arrhythmias (VT)  
  R – Recurrent syncope  
  D – Dilated cardiomyopathy  
  I – Inflammatory myocardial uptake on PET  
  A – ACE elevation  
  C – Cardiac MRI with LGE

- **Scadding stages:** "0-1-2-3-4, BHL first, then fibrosis at the door"  
  0 = normal  
  1 = lymph nodes  
  2 = nodes + infiltrates  
  3 = infiltrates alone  
  4 = fibrosis/honeycombing

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **BHL in an otherwise well young adult + erythema nodosum = Löfgren syndrome = excellent prognosis. No need to biopsy if classic.**
2. **Negative TST (or IGRA) in a patient with BHL and uveitis strongly points to sarcoidosis over TB (but beware anergy – false negative TST can occur in sarcoidosis itself).**
3. **ACE level >2x upper limit is highly suggestive of active disease; falling ACE on therapy indicates response.**
4. **Scar sarcoidosis: old scars (from surgery, tattoos, piercings) become raised, red, and tender – biopsy that scar for easy diagnosis.**
5. **Cardiac MRI with mid-myocardial LGE is diagnostic of cardiac sarcoidosis; do not misinterpret as infarct pattern (subendocardial).**
6. **Hypercalcemia in sarcoid responds to hydroxychloroquine because it inhibits 1α-hydroxylase – additive to steroids.**
7. **Pulmonary function test decline of ≥10% in FVC or DLCO over 6 months warrants treatment even if symptoms mild.**

**Common mistakes:**
- Ordering bronchoscopy without checking platelets and INR (biopsy risk bleeding).
- Mistaking lymphoma for sarcoidosis – lymphoma nodes are asymmetrical, can be painful, and often B symptoms present.
- Assuming all granulomas are sarcoidosis – remember TB, fungal, beryllium, Crohn's, Wegener's (GPA), and foreign body reactions.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/attending):**
- Suspected **cardiac sarcoidosis** (syncope, new AV block, ventricular arrhythmia) – needs urgent cardiology and initiation of steroids.
- **Acute visual loss** or severe uveitis – ophthalmology same day.
- **Hypercalcemia** Ca >3.0 mmol/L – requires IV fluids, monitoring, endocrine input.
- **Acute respiratory decline** with falling SpO2 <92% on room air – rule out infection, pulmonary embolism, sarcoid exacerbation.
- **New focal neurological deficit** – urgent MRI brain + neurology consult.
- **Pulmonary fibrosis** with suspected pulmonary hypertension – echocardiogram, right heart catheterization consideration.

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **Cardiac arrest** due to ventricular arrhythmia from cardiac sarcoid – immediate resuscitation, ICD evaluation.
- **Complete heart block** with syncope or hemodynamic instability – emergency pacing (transcutaneous, then temporary transvenous).
- **Respiratory failure** (PaO2 <60 mmHg on >60% FiO2) – consider non-invasive ventilation, then intubation if needed.
- **Hypercalcemic crisis** with GCS <13 or seizures – ICU for aggressive hydration, bisphosphonates, dialysis may be required.
- **Anaphylaxis** (rare) – but if giving infliximab.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** → immediate senior review + possible ICU referral.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (if sepsis or cardiac cause) → urgent escalation.
- **GCS < 13** → protect airway, CT head, ICU.
- **ECG: second-degree Mobitz II or complete AV block** → cardiology consult for pacing within 1 hour.
- **LVEF ≤35%** on echocardiogram → consider ICD (cardiology decision).

*Remember: Sarcoidosis can mimic many emergencies – always ask "could this be cardiac/neuro/renal sarcoid?" before dismissing as idiopathic.*

rawMarkdown:
## Sarcoidosis
**One-liner:** Multisystem granulomatous disease of unknown cause, classically in young Black women and Scandinavians, often with lung/hilar involvement but can affect any organ.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 32-year-old Black female presents with a 2-week history of dry cough, breathlessness on climbing stairs, and vague chest discomfort. She also complains of red, painful eyes and tender red bumps on her shins. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) and clear lung fields. She is otherwise well and not febrile. On examination, you note erythema nodosum, bilateral anterior uveitis, and no crackles on auscultation. ACE level is raised. She mentions she recently stopped taking her contraceptive pill, which is irrelevant.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**
- **Demographics (young Black female)** → Sarcoidosis has highest incidence in Black Americans and people of Scandinavian descent; female predominance; peak age 20-40.
- **Dry cough + breathlessness** → Interstitial lung involvement or airway irritation from granulomatous inflammation; early stage often no crackles.
- **Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) on CXR** → Classic and near-pathognomonic; symmetrical "potato nodes" on CXR.
- **Erythema nodosum (EN)** → Tender red nodules on shins – a common acute presentation (Löfgren syndrome when combined with BHL and arthralgia/uveitis).
- **Red painful eyes (anterior uveitis)** → Granulomatous inflammation of uveal tract; can be bilateral.
- **Raised ACE** → Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme elevated in ~60% of active sarcoidosis; not specific but supportive.
- **No fever, well appearance** → Unlike infection; sarcoidosis often presents in a relatively well patient despite significant radiological findings.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Cardiac sarcoidosis** → Young adult with syncope, heart block, or ventricular arrhythmias; often misdiagnosed as myocarditis or inherited cardiomyopathy. Look for late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI.
- **Neurosarcoidosis** → Cranial nerve palsy (especially facial nerve), meningeal enhancement, or hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction. Can mimic multiple sclerosis or stroke.
- **Isolated skin sarcoidosis** → Chronic plaques, lupus pernio (red/purple nodules on nose/ears), or scar sarcoid (infiltration of old scars). Patient may have no other symptoms – easy to miss.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory disorder characterized by non-caseating epithelioid granulomas in affected organs. The lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes are involved in >90% of cases, but virtually any organ can be affected (eyes, skin, heart, nervous system, liver, spleen, bones). The cause remains unknown, though it's thought to be an exaggerated immune response to an environmental antigen in genetically susceptible individuals.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Annual incidence ~10-40/100,000 in the US; higher in Black populations (3x-4x). It's a common cause of interstitial lung disease.
- **Stakes:** Most patients (~60%) have self-limiting disease, but up to 30% develop chronic progressive sarcoidosis leading to pulmonary fibrosis, pulmonary hypertension, or end-organ damage (e.g., cardiac sarcoidosis causing sudden death).
- **Pitfalls:** Misdiagnosis as tuberculosis or lymphoma is common due to similar presentation with BHL and granulomas. Conversely, missing cardiac sarcoidosis can be fatal. Also, corticosteroids are mainstay but overtreatment can cause harm; many cases resolve spontaneously.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:** Sarcoidosis begins when an unknown antigen (possible triggers: mycobacteria, propionibacteria, inorganic dusts) is presented to CD4+ T-cells in genetically predisposed individuals. The CD4+ T-cells (primarily Th1/Th17) release interferon-gamma and IL-2, which recruit macrophages. Macrophages accumulate and differentiate into epithelioid cells, fusing into multinucleated giant cells, forming **non-caseating granulomas** — the histological hallmark. Unlike tuberculosis, there is no central necrosis (caseation) in sarcoid granulomas.

**Mechanism -> Clinical signs:**
- **Granuloma formation in lungs** → Compromises alveolar architecture, causing restrictive lung disease, dry cough, dyspnea. Early: no crackles because granulomas are interstitial, not alveolar filling.
- **Hilar and mediastinal lymph node involvement** → BHL on CXR. Granulomas obstruct lymphatic drainage, causing node enlargement. Symmetrical because both lungs are exposed to same antigens.
- **Erythema nodosum** → Immune complex deposition or direct granulomatous inflammation in subcutaneous fat; mediated by TNF-α and other cytokines. Typically acute onset in Löfgren syndrome.
- **Uveitis** → Direct granulomatous infiltration of uveal tract; anterior uveitis is most common, causing photophobia, redness, pain. Granulomas disrupt the blood-ocular barrier.
- **Elevated ACE** → Granulomatous epithelioid cells produce ACE; serum levels correlate with total body granuloma burden but not specific (also ↑ in TB, Gaucher disease).
- **Anergy (negative tuberculin skin test)** → Paradoxical; sarcoidosis patients often have diminished delayed-type hypersensitivity (due to Th1 compartment quiescence?), while simultaneously having hyperactive granulomatous inflammation.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The well-appearing patient with BHL, EN, and uveitis reflects a robust but organized granulomatous reaction – hence "Löfgren syndrome" often has excellent prognosis. Conversely, chronic progressive sarcoidosis (fibrotic lung, lupus pernio) suggests dysregulated inflammation with fibrosis.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**Most common cause: Idiopathic** – by definition, no definite trigger identified. But associations exist.

| Category | Specific trigger | Stem clue / presentation |
|----------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | No known cause (majority) | Classic BHL + EN in young woman |
| **Genetic / Familial** | HLA-DRB1*0301, BTNL2 variants | Scandinavian ancestry, family history of sarcoidosis |
| **Infectious (proposed)** | Mycobacteria (M. tuberculosis, M. avium) | Patient from high TB area – but sarcoid granulomas lack caseation and stain negative for acid-fast bacilli. "Fungal" triggers also suggested (P. acnes) |
| **Environmental / Occupational** | Exposures: pine pollen, beryllium, aluminum, zirconium | Occupational history – be aware of berylliosis (disease mimics sarcoidosis but specifically from beryllium; lymphocytes show proliferation to beryllium on blood test) |
| **Drug-induced** | TNF-α inhibitors (paradoxical sarcoidosis), immune checkpoint inhibitors | New-onset sarcoid-like reactions in patients on anti-TNF or PD-1 inhibitors |

**Key point:** In clinical practice, you rarely identify a specific trigger – hence "idiopathic" remains the default. However, if the patient has occupational exposure to beryllium, you must differentiate chronic beryllium disease (CBD) from sarcoidosis via a beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT) – CBD is indistinguishable histologically.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** No single gold standard. Diagnosis requires: (1) compatible clinical presentation, (2) histologic evidence of non-caseating granulomas (from biopsy of most accessible affected site), (3) exclusion of other causes of granulomas (TB, fungal, berylliosis, etc.). The **WASOG criteria** (World Association of Sarcoidosis and Other Granulomatous Diseases) classify as "definite" if histology + typical imaging.

**Chest X-ray (first line):** Shows Scadding stages:
- **Stage 0:** Normal
- **Stage I:** BHL only
- **Stage II:** BHL + interstitial lung disease (reticulonodular opacities)
- **Stage III:** Interstitial lung disease without BHL (woody fibrosis)
- **Stage IV:** Pulmonary fibrosis (honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis)

**Pattern recognition:** Stage I is classic in acute Löfgren – good prognosis (60-80% spontaneous resolution). Stage IV indicates irreversible fibrosis, poor prognosis.

**High-resolution CT chest:** Shows perilymphatic nodules (along fissures and bronchovascular bundles) – "cluster of grapes" sign. Also ground-glass opacities, hilar lymphadenopathy. Helps guide biopsy.

**Biopsy (essential for definitive diagnosis):** Options:
- Bronchoscopy with endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS)-guided TBNA of hilar nodes (first choice, high yield)
- Transbronchial lung biopsy (yield ~70%)
- Endobronchial biopsy (if visible mucosal lesions)
- Skin biopsy of EN or lupus pernio (easy if skin involved)
- Conjunctival biopsy (if ocular symptoms)

**Histology:** Non-caseating epithelioid granulomas with Schaumann bodies (laminated concretions) and asteroid bodies (stellate inclusions) – classic but not specific.

**Serum ACE:** Elevated in ~60% of active sarcoidosis. Not diagnostic alone (↑ in TB, lymphoma, Gaucher, hyperthyroidism). Useful for monitoring disease activity – falling ACE may indicate response to therapy.

**Calcium metabolism:** 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D is produced by granulomas (macrophages have 1α-hydroxylase). This leads to hypercalciuria in up to 40% and hypercalcemia in ~10%. **Check serum calcium and 24-hour urine calcium.** High calcium can cause nephrocalcinosis.

**Pulmonary function tests (PFTs):** Restrictive pattern (↓ FVC, ↓ TLC, normal or ↑ FEV1/FVC). DLCO may be reduced early due to parenchymal involvement.

**Other labs:** 
- Elevated ESR/CRP (non-specific)
- Lymphocytopenia (common)
- Hypergammaglobulinemia (polyclonal)
- Negative ANCA, negative ANA (unless co-existing autoimmune disease)

**Single finding that confirms:** Non-caseating granuloma on biopsy + negative cultures and stains for AFB/fungi **in the right clinical context**.

**Single finding that rules out:** Positive AFB smear/culture from same sample (TB) or identification of beryllium-specific hypersensitivity.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – if severe/exacerbation:**
- Usually not an emergency unless:
  - **Hypercalcemic crisis** (Ca >3.5 mmol/L, altered mental state, ECG short QT): IV normal saline 1-2L, bisphosphonates (pamidronate 60-90mg IV over 2-4h) consultation with endocrinology.
  - **Cardiac sarcoidosis with ventricular arrhythmia or heart block:** Immediate cardiology consult, monitor in CCU, consider temporary pacing if high-grade AV block. No specific anti-inflammatory within first hour unless electrophysiology guided.
- **Acute uveitis:** Urgent ophthalmology referral – topical steroids (prednisolone acetate 1% drops q1-2h) + cycloplegics.

**Ward (24-72h) – for newly diagnosed or exacerbation:**
- **Indications for systemic therapy:** Symptomatic disease (cough, dyspnea, fatigue, significant eye/skin/heart involvement) or progressive pulmonary function decline.
- **First line:** Prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day (max 40-80mg) oral for 4-6 weeks, then taper gradually over 6-12 months. Steroids reduce granuloma burden and suppress cytokine release.
- **Why not start immediate steroids in all?** Many patients remit spontaneously (especially Stage I). Unnecessary steroids cause weight gain, osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts. Use shared decision-making.
- **Other acute measures:** Calcium restricted diet if hypercalcemia; IVF if high calcium. Monitor renal function.
- **When to add second-line:**
  - Refractory disease / steroid side effects: **Methotrexate** 7.5-25mg weekly oral/subcut (with folic acid 5mg weekly). Check LFTs, FBC, creatinine.
  - **Hydroxychloroquine** 200-400mg/day for skin/calcium issues.
  - **Infliximab** 5mg/kg IV at weeks 0,2,6 then q8w for severe refractory.

**Long-term (months to years):**
- Taper steroids to lowest effective dose (often ≤10mg prednisolone/day). Continue methotrexate or other steroid-sparing agent.
- **Pulmonary fibrosis:** Pulmonary rehabilitation, long-term oxygen if PaO₂ <55mmHg, consider lung transplant if end-stage.
- **Cardiac sarcoidosis:** Long-term immunosuppression (steroids ± mycophenolate) plus implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) if significant arrhythmias or LVEF <35%.
- **Ocular sarcoidosis:** Long-term topical steroids, sometimes systemic. Biannual slit-lamp exams.
- **Hypercalcemia:** Avoid vitamin D supplements, limit sun exposure, consider hydroxychloroquine (reduces 1,25-(OH)2D production).
- **Monitoring:** Every 6-12 months: CXR, PFTs, ACE, calcium, ophthalmology review. Image for cardiac/neuro symptoms.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- Do NOT start steroids for asymptomatic Stage I BHL alone – 50-70% resolve spontaneously.
- Do NOT use TNF-α inhibitors (especially etanercept) – they can actually cause sarcoidosis flares! Infliximab is preferred for refractory cases.
- Do NOT stop steroids abruptly – leads to rebound granulomatous inflammation.
- Do NOT assume a raised ACE confirms sarcoidosis in a patient with chest symptoms from lymphoma or TB – always get tissue.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Sarcoidosis | Tuberculosis | Lymphoma | Berylliosis |
|---------|-------------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Usually no chest pain (if pulmonary) | Pleuritic chest pain possible | Chest pain rare unless large nodes | Similar to sarcoidosis; no pain |
| **Exam** | Crackles late; erythema nodosum; uveitis; clubbing uncommon | Night sweats, fever, weight loss (classic TB symptoms); crackles | Supraclavicular nodes (Rubber, fixed); hepatosplenomegaly; fever | Crackles with interstitial disease; no EN |
| **CXR** | BHL symmetrical "potato nodes"; interstitial nodules | Usually unilateral hilar or paratracheal node; apical cavities | Bilateral but often asymmetrical hilar/mediastinal nodes; ± pulmonary masses | BHL and reticulonodular opacities identical to sarcoidosis |
| **Labs** | ACE ↑; Ca ↑; negative TST/IGRA | Positive TST/IGRA (if not anergic); sputum AFB positive | Elevated LDH; flow cytometry shows clonal lymphocytes | BeLPT positive; ACE may be ↑ |
| **Key diff** | Non-caseating granuloma + negative cultures | Caseating granuloma + AFB+ | Lymphoma cells (Reed-Sternberg in Hodgkin) | Positive beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test; occupational history |

**Additional mimics:** 
- **Hypersensitivity pneumonitis:** No EN, no uveitis, no BHL; history of bird/occupational dust exposure; precipitating antibodies.
- **Churg-Strauss (EGPA):** Eosinophilia, asthma, vasculitic rash, p-ANCA positive.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Pulmonary fibrosis (Stage IV):**
- **Presentation:** Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, digital clubbing (late). CXR shows honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis.
- **Key signs:** Bilateral basal crackles, reduced FVC and DLCO.
- **What to do:** High-resolution CT to assess fibrosis extent. Start long-term oxygen if hypoxic. Lung transplant evaluation. Stop smoking.
- **If untreated:** Irreversible respiratory failure, pulmonary hypertension, cor pulmonale, death.

**2. Cardiac sarcoidosis:**
- **Presentation:** Palpitations, syncope, heart failure, sudden cardiac death (may be first presentation). ECG: AV block (any degree), bundle branch block, ventricular ectopy, Q waves simulating MI.
- **Key signs:** Abnormal cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement (mid-myocardial, not subendocardial). PET shows focal FDG uptake.
- **What to do:** Start high-dose steroids (prednisolone 1mg/kg) + methotrexate. Insert ICD if LVEF ≤35% or significant arrhythmias.
- **If missed:** Sudden death is common – cardiac sarcoidosis is a leading cause of death in sarcoidosis patients.

**3. Neurosarcoidosis:**
- **Presentation:** Cranial nerve palsy (CN VII most common), headache, seizures, spinal cord symptoms. MRI: leptomeningeal enhancement, periventricular white matter lesions.
- **Key signs:** CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, ACE may be ↑. Rule out infectious meningitis.
- **What to do:** High-dose steroids (IV methylprednisolone 1g/day for 5 days) then oral taper. Add immunosuppressant (methotrexate, mycophenolate).
- **If missed:** Permanent cranial nerve deficits, hydrocephalus, myelopathy.

**4. Hypercalcemia / Nephrolithiasis:**
- **Presentation:** Polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, confusion. Stones may present as colicky flank pain.
- **Key signs:** Serum Ca >2.6 mmol/L; urine Ca high; nephrocalcinosis on ultrasound.
- **What to do:** Hydration, bisphosphonates if severe, stop vitamin D, consider hydroxychloroquine.
- **If missed:** Renal failure, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.

**5. Ocular sarcoidosis (chronic):**
- **Presentation:** Gradual vision loss, glaucoma, cataracts. Posterior uveitis can cause macular edema.
- **What to do:** Regular ophthalmology follow-up. Topical/systemic steroids. Consider anti-TNF if refractory.
- **If missed:** Blindness.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **LÖFGREN syndrome** (good prognosis):  
  L – Lymphadenopathy (bilateral hilar)  
  Ö – Ögon (eye = uveitis)  
  F – Fever (low-grade)  
  G – Granulomatous arthritis (ankle pain/swelling)  
  R – Rash (erythema nodosum)  
  EN – Erythema Nodosum (already covered)

- **CARDIAC sarcoidosis clues:**  
  C – Conduction defects (AV block)  
  A – Arrhythmias (VT)  
  R – Recurrent syncope  
  D – Dilated cardiomyopathy  
  I – Inflammatory myocardial uptake on PET  
  A – ACE elevation  
  C – Cardiac MRI with LGE

- **Scadding stages:** "0-1-2-3-4, BHL first, then fibrosis at the door"  
  0 = normal  
  1 = lymph nodes  
  2 = nodes + infiltrates  
  3 = infiltrates alone  
  4 = fibrosis/honeycombing

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **BHL in an otherwise well young adult + erythema nodosum = Löfgren syndrome = excellent prognosis. No need to biopsy if classic.**
2. **Negative TST (or IGRA) in a patient with BHL and uveitis strongly points to sarcoidosis over TB (but beware anergy – false negative TST can occur in sarcoidosis itself).**
3. **ACE level >2x upper limit is highly suggestive of active disease; falling ACE on therapy indicates response.**
4. **Scar sarcoidosis: old scars (from surgery, tattoos, piercings) become raised, red, and tender – biopsy that scar for easy diagnosis.**
5. **Cardiac MRI with mid-myocardial LGE is diagnostic of cardiac sarcoidosis; do not misinterpret as infarct pattern (subendocardial).**
6. **Hypercalcemia in sarcoid responds to hydroxychloroquine because it inhibits 1α-hydroxylase – additive to steroids.**
7. **Pulmonary function test decline of ≥10% in FVC or DLCO over 6 months warrants treatment even if symptoms mild.**

**Common mistakes:**
- Ordering bronchoscopy without checking platelets and INR (biopsy risk bleeding).
- Mistaking lymphoma for sarcoidosis – lymphoma nodes are asymmetrical, can be painful, and often B symptoms present.
- Assuming all granulomas are sarcoidosis – remember TB, fungal, beryllium, Crohn's, Wegener's (GPA), and foreign body reactions.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar/attending):**
- Suspected **cardiac sarcoidosis** (syncope, new AV block, ventricular arrhythmia) – needs urgent cardiology and initiation of steroids.
- **Acute visual loss** or severe uveitis – ophthalmology same day.
- **Hypercalcemia** Ca >3.0 mmol/L – requires IV fluids, monitoring, endocrine input.
- **Acute respiratory decline** with falling SpO2 <92% on room air – rule out infection, pulmonary embolism, sarcoid exacerbation.
- **New focal neurological deficit** – urgent MRI brain + neurology consult.
- **Pulmonary fibrosis** with suspected pulmonary hypertension – echocardiogram, right heart catheterization consideration.

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- **Cardiac arrest** due to ventricular arrhythmia from cardiac sarcoid – immediate resuscitation, ICD evaluation.
- **Complete heart block** with syncope or hemodynamic instability – emergency pacing (transcutaneous, then temporary transvenous).
- **Respiratory failure** (PaO2 <60 mmHg on >60% FiO2) – consider non-invasive ventilation, then intubation if needed.
- **Hypercalcemic crisis** with GCS <13 or seizures – ICU for aggressive hydration, bisphosphonates, dialysis may be required.
- **Anaphylaxis** (rare) – but if giving infliximab.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 7** → immediate senior review + possible ICU referral.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (if sepsis or cardiac cause) → urgent escalation.
- **GCS < 13** → protect airway, CT head, ICU.
- **ECG: second-degree Mobitz II or complete AV block** → cardiology consult for pacing within 1 hour.
- **LVEF ≤35%** on echocardiogram → consider ICD (cardiology decision).

*Remember: Sarcoidosis can mimic many emergencies – always ask "could this be cardiac/neuro/renal sarcoid?" before dismissing as idiopathic.*
